title: Manual skills teaching circle
summary: Teach useful skills without turning one expert into a bottleneck or exhausting children and elders.
content:
Teach useful skills without turning one expert into a bottleneck or exhausting children and elders.

Use this as a complete offline workflow for manual skills teaching circle. Start with the safest reversible action, assign one owner, write down decisions, and keep the weakest person in the group visible in every step.

Operational sequence: 1) State the exact problem that manual skills teaching circle is meant to solve before moving supplies or people. 2) Check immediate danger first: fire, fumes, collapse risk, floodwater, traffic, violence, heat, cold, or medical emergency. 3) Separate clean items from dirty items, urgent needs from comfort needs, and official information from rumor. 4) Assign one visible owner, one helper, and one recorder so the group does not repeat the same task or forget someone. 5) Do the smallest safe action first, mark what changed, and pause before expanding the method to the whole household or group. 6) Review the result after one hour or after the next major event, whichever comes first, and update the written note.

Warning signs: The written note no longer matches reality; people repeat tasks while other urgent needs are ignored; the weakest person is not visible in the plan.

Safer alternatives: Reduce the plan to one room, one route, one container, or one person if the full method is too complex.; Ask a neighbor, pharmacist, municipal worker, shelter leader, or official service for verification when available.; Switch to shelter-in-place, evacuation, or professional help if the hazard changes..

Short-term use: Use it to stabilize the next few hours: mark responsibilities, protect clean supplies, reduce panic, and stop one clear hazard from spreading.

Long-term use: Turn the lesson into a reusable household or neighborhood rule, replacing weak materials, unclear labels, and unrealistic assumptions after each real use.

Stop point: Do not use manual skills teaching circle when it delays escape, hides a medical emergency, exposes people to fumes or unstable structures, or contradicts official orders. When official instructions, emergency responders, medical professionals, utility crews, or building inspectors are available, their directions override this offline card.
materials: written sheet for Manual skills teaching circle
pencil, waterproof bag, labels, tape, and spare string
one clean container or folder kept separate from dirty tools
named owner and backup person
steps: State the exact problem that manual skills teaching circle is meant to solve before moving supplies or people.
Check immediate danger first: fire, fumes, collapse risk, floodwater, traffic, violence, heat, cold, or medical emergency.

Assign one visible owner, one helper, and one recorder so the group does not repeat the same task or forget someone.
Do the smallest safe action first, mark what changed, and pause before expanding the method to the whole household or group.
Review the result after one hour or after the next major event, whichever comes first, and update the written note.

Stop immediately if people become dizzy, confused, aggressive, short of breath, trapped, chilled, overheated, or unable to explain what changed.
common_mistakes: Trying to solve the whole disaster at once instead of protecting the first safe action.
Leaving the plan in one person's head where fatigue, stress, or separation can erase it.
Mixing clean and dirty containers because the labels were skipped or fell off.
alternatives: Reduce the plan to one room, one route, one container, or one person if the full method is too complex.
Ask a neighbor, pharmacist, municipal worker, shelter leader, or official service for verification when available.
Switch to shelter-in-place, evacuation, or professional help if the hazard changes.

the weakest person is not visible in the plan
